misconceptionSummary: Students do not understand why the decimal place count method works — they apply it mechanically and lose track of the size of the answer.

visuals.ruleText: Multiplying by 10 scales up; the decimal method undoes that scaling

steps.narration: The decimal method works by scaling. Three point four is thirty-four divided by ten. So three point four times seven equals thirty-four times seven, then divided by ten. Multiplying as whole numbers and then dividing by ten is exactly what placing the decimal point one place from the right achieves.

Three point four times seven equals thirty-four divided by ten times seven, which equals thirty-four times seven divided by ten. Thirty-four times seven is two hundred and thirty-eight. Dividing by ten gives twenty-three point eight.

Before calculating, estimate: three times seven is twenty-one. The answer should be close to twenty-one — around twenty to twenty-five. Twenty-three point eight fits. Two point three eight is too small, two hundred and thirty-eight is far too large.

visuals.label: = (34 × 7) ÷ 10 — decimal place is the ÷10 in disguise

steps.narration: Twenty-three point eight. Estimating first gives a target range, and the scaling argument explains why the decimal place method is not just a rule — it is dividing by ten hidden in notation.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: placing the decimal by feel rather than by counting. Estimate first — 3.4 × 7 ≈ 21 — then use the count: 1 decimal place in the question means 1 in the answer: 23.8.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Estimate first to know the right scale. Then the decimal-place count confirms where the point goes. The method is really multiplying as integers and dividing by the appropriate power of ten.
